item.title: Friday Squid Blogging: “Squidbleed” Vulnerability
item.description: pIn a rare combined cybersecurity/squid post, a twenty-nine-year-old a href="https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/29-year-old-squid-proxy-bug-squidbleed.html"squid proxy bug/a can leak HTTP requests./ppAs usual, you can also use this squid post to talk about the security stories in the news that I have

item.title: RT by @ylecun: 🎯 If Trump were actively trying to destroy American advantages, to create chaos and strife that he and his ilk could exploit, what would he do differently?
item.description: p🎯 If Trump were actively trying to destroy American advantages, to create chaos and strife that he and his ilk could exploit, what would he do differently?/phr /blockquotebYann LeCun (@ylecun)/bppTypical NYT understatement for what is an apocalyptic destruction of the American innovation ecosystem.

Directional indicators represent each project's ranking positioning since last update:br /br /➡️ 1) a href="https://nitter.net/chainlink" title="Chainlink"@chainlink/a a href="https://nitter.net/search?f=tweets&amp;q=%23LINK"$LINK/a

item.title: RT by @Dinosn: Our research team is back looking at CVE-2026-47291 - a CVSS 9.8 RCE bug in #Windows HTTP.sys. They show root cause and look at why detection may be just as hard as exploitation. https:
item.description: pOur research team is back looking at CVE-2026-47291 - a CVSS 9.8 RCE bug in a href="https://nitter.net/search?f=tweets&amp;q=%23Windows"#Windows/a HTTP.sys. They show root cause and look at why detection may be just as hard as exploitation. a href="https://www.zerodayinitiative.com/blog/2026/7/9/cv

item.title: R to @l2beat: Is there another property we should evaluate? Which privacy protocol should we look into next? 🔎 Let us know! 💗
item.description: pIs there another property we should evaluate?br /br /Which privacy protocol should we look into next? 🔎br /br /Let us know! 💗/p

item.title: R to @l2beat: We use these properties to map protocol guarantees to CROPS, but some protocol guarantees, like privacy, only go as far as your own opsec. That's why we've also outlined privacy best pra
item.description: pWe use these properties to map protocol guarantees to CROPS, but some protocol guarantees, like privacy, only go as far as your own opsec.br /br /That's why we've also outlined privacy best practices: a href="https://l2beat.com/publications/privacy-best-practices"l2beat.com/publications/priv…/a/phr

item.title: R to @l2beat: The Admin view key/Privacy column maps to Privacy. Can anyone see through the shield? A protocol can hide activity from the public while still exposing it to a privileged party, often fo
item.description: pThe Admin view key/Privacy column maps to Privacy.br /br /Can anyone see through the shield? A protocol can hide activity from the public while still exposing it to a privileged party, often for compliance.br /br /a href="https://nitter.net/TornadoCash" title="🌪️ Tornado.cash 🌪️"@TornadoCash/a, a h

item.title: R to @l2beat: CROPS = Censorship (and capture) resistance, Openness, Privacy and Security. We don't claim to measure all of it - we don't score audits, for example. We surface concrete properties that
item.description: pCROPS = Censorship (and capture) resistance, Openness, Privacy and Security.br /br /We don't claim to measure all of it - we don't score audits, for example. br /We surface concrete properties that show where users still need to trust a protocol or a privileged actor./p

item.title: We built our Privacy Dashboard to make some of the CROPS principles easier to inspect and compare across privacy protocols. Here's how each column on the page maps to the principles 👇
item.description: pWe built our Privacy Dashboard to make some of the CROPS principles easier to inspect and compare across privacy protocols.br /br /Here's how each column on the page maps to the principles 👇/pimg src="https://nitter.net/pic/media%2FHM3sGpTXgAAAENP.jpg" /

item.title: Microsoft Reins in RoguePlanet Zero-Day Threat
item.description: The researcher known as &quot;Nightmare-Eclipse&quot; published a proof-of-concept (PoC) exploit for the Windows Defender vulnerability in early June after dropping several other Microsoft zero-days.

item.title: RT by @polymutex: Truly a sad day for privacy and democracy But adding some nuance here. What passed is an extension of Chat Control 1.0, i.e voluntary scanning of non-encrypted messages for known abu
item.description: pTruly a sad day for privacy and democracy br /br /But adding some nuance here. What passed is an extension of Chat Control 1.0, i.e voluntary scanning of non-encrypted messages for known abuse materials.br /br /Its not the same as Chat Control 2.0, nor a blank warrant or breaking of E2EE chats.br /

item.title: R to @l2beat: This is still early days for Zama, the decentralization roadmap is still ahead, and we'll keep tracking it as it lands! Explore the details here: https://l2beat.com/privacy/projects/zama

item.title: 'GodDamn' Ransomware Uses BYOVD to Smite US Companies
item.description: Microsoft co-signed a malicious kernel driver, and now it's being used to kill security software in ransomware attacks.

item.title: The security of a trusted setup depends on its participants: if all of them colluded, arbitrary proofs could be forged. A single honest contributor makes that impossible. You can support Privacy Pools
item.description: pThe security of a trusted setup depends on its participants: if all of them colluded, arbitrary proofs could be forged. A single honest contributor makes that impossible.br /br /You can support Privacy Pools today by contributing your entropy and spreading the word to get the participant count up 👇
